speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape seven, our survivor, Shoshana Kahn. Today is June 10, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in the neighboring kibbutz by the rabbi in Gevat. And we didn't have a glass. Somebody had to go to the infirmary and get one of those things that they make urine tests. That was the only glass they could afford to lose. It wouldn't break. They had to step a lot on it. But we got married anyhow. We still got married. And then we had kind of a little reception. We all sat on orange crates-- we didn't have furniture-- on orange crates. And we were singing and ate oranges. It was the war. All right. And we all got married on-- on the 14th of November-- yes, the 14th of November in '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could I ask you that question. Who was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dror, my son. And we stayed-- I stayed in the kibbutz about 12 years. And in '46, I came to the States, because I always want to come to America, have a white picket fence at last. But I'm very close to my friends in the kibbutz. They all come to visit me. I am the-- I am [INAUDIBLE] Shoshana, the hotel for the whole kibbutzim. I have-- in all the kibbutzim, I have friends. In the Alonim, we are still close. In Sarid, they are still close. In HaGoshrim, we are still close friends. I have really, really close friends. I have [INAUDIBLE] in-- in the kibbutzim. And then, in conclusion, I want to tell you. I am so-- I am really sad that this tragedy has happened, of course. And it is horrible for me to have-- having-- having lost my little-- my little sisters in such terrible circumstances and all that. But if it had to happen, really, if it was in the-- in the stars that that should have happened to our Jewish people, I'm kind of grateful that I was there. And I'm grateful I survived. But me having been there and having seen that all and lived through it, it's enriched my life in many ways. First of all, I learned about people. I learned about people who were little people and grew to tremendous-- with courage to tremendous heroes. And I-- I-- I know I can be happy to be Jewish, because when I watch the Germans in certain situations, I was saying to myself, thank God I am not a German. I am not in there. And thank God I'm Jewish. I am lucky I'm not a German. And when I meet now my German friends, young German friends-- I have a few of them-- I feel sorry for them. When [? Zozanna ?] says, you cannot imagine, [? Shoshantia, ?] what-- the shame I have. And she's young. She has nothing to do with it. Every Easter, to be in New York, constantly to be reminded of what-- what people did. You-- you have-- you have no idea. And I feel really sorry for her, instead of her feeling sorry for me, of [NON-ENGLISH] and so on. I feel bad for them. Because they have it on their conscience. They have it on their history. We don't so far. We are lucky. And then another thing-- I mean, to me, the grass is greener than to everybody. The food is tastier than to everybody. I'll never forget-- getting up in the morning and having a Frigidaire with food is the ultimate luxury. Having a warm bed is somebody-- I or my friends can appreciate to have a roof over one's head, to have grandchildren. I mean, I'll never forget that. There was the first time I took Ezi-- Ezra is my grandson-- to the Mostly Mozart. And they were playing Mozart. I will crying that, look at me, sitting in New York at Lincoln Center with this glorious grandson on my side. Hey, I made it! I made it! You know, other people just go to a concert. And this is-- everything is doubly, tripley precious and beautiful. So I have, really, a very rich life.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is-- it's strange. It is, again, oxymoronic. But those are the facts. Every-- every bite tastes better. And I am forever grateful to the people who helped. I am forever grateful to every non-Jew whose-- who put his life on the-- on the-- at risk, to save a total stranger-- total stranger. And I think about it. How would we have behaved? I don't know if I would have done the same things they did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Poles have very bad reputations. They're very anti-Semitic historically. But it came to the nitty-gritty, they were the ones who had an underground-- with-- had an organization called the Council for the Aid for the Jews. There was an organization of-- of doctors who did nothing else but visiting Jews and hiding and treating them. They did plastic surgery on noses. They did plastic surgery to reverse a bris. The-- the-- the government in exile in London supported the Council for the Aid for the Jews, monthly with money. People in hiding could go to a certain doctor, wait in the waiting room, and-- and-- and called in and get a check. Some people had to be transported 75 different hiding places. And there, again, to be a Polish person having hidden a Jew-- got-- their punishment was incredible. They were-- used to take out the child-- children first to the market. Everybody watch it. They executed children, then the parents, then the neighbors, and so on. And that was a law. And they still did it. And I think we should never forget those people who did so much for us when they didn't have to. They just could have kept quiet. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back a little bit, how did you support yourselves when you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked-- I went to school and studied Swedish massage, because I didn't know English that well.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spend your free time now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I read. I go to theater a couple of times a week. I go to the concerts a couple of times a week. And I-- I play bridge. Yeah, that's about what I do. I go to classes. And went to the New School a lot of times. Visual arts is very interesting to me. I love museums and galleries. I mean, I plan to go to Philadelphia to see the [? son. ?] I go to every exhibit possible. I-- I do what my father-in-law told me. Surround yourself with beautiful things, my dear Shoshana. The ugly come on their own. And that's what I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think now of Latvia or your home, what comes to mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: The beach.

speaker: interviewee
text: The strawberries. My mom's jasmine and dahlias and her gefilte fish. What gefilte fish they did. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The school, [? Lentian ?] and Hilda and [? Ritan. ?] And my teachers-- that lovely school. We had such a good time. And [? Vitinka, ?] and [? Micki, ?] and [? Ossi. ?] Did you ever question the existence of God?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't question. I don't believe in God. So I don't question. I have a-- i-- I think-- oh, no. I don't want to say that. It's not for the tape. No, I have a problem with God. I will-- I meet him, I meet my maker-- I am going to tell him off. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The children-- how could that have happened? How could that have happened? The little children-- how could he have let it happen?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think gave you the strength to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sense of humor, probably, and chutzpah. I was very lucky. I was at the right time, at the right age, with the right looks, and the right personality. It has all-- it all added to-- to survival. There are no-- there are no miracles. I mean, I had it easier than lots of other people from that respect. So that probably helped a lot. And this-- this conviction that it won't happen to me-- this doesn't happen to me. It happens to anybody, but not me. And so I never was really afraid. That's why I could play the-- the-- the chutzpah and the games. And acting was always something I wanted to do. So I acted Veronica. And I acted Catherine. And I acted this one or that one. So I acted [? Jean ?] Simone, so that they-- then they see that I was over. It's true. It's true, I was a pretty good actress. And I-- and-- and my knowledge of-- of German. I mean, had I not known German that well, that very well, I could have made a boo-boo. I could have made a mistake and said, I come from Stuttgart or Munich or what. I listened good how those people talked and picked a-- a-- a city which doesn't sound like anyone else. So that is clever for-- for a young girl. I mean, Catherine would have done that. I had it all laid out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any dreams or nightmares as a result of your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- only nightmares I have are very pleasant ones. I'm-- and the nightmares-- no. I am always in a-- in a very safe spot, where things are going outside. And I am sitting cozy, surrounded with sweaters, endless sweaters. I love sweaters. All cozy warm sweaters all over me, hundreds of them. No, my only nightmares are that I am having exams in school. Everybody hands in the paper. And I don't even know what they're talking about. That is my nightmare. And then I wake up in sweat and thank God I'm old, and I don't have to do tests anymore. And I [INAUDIBLE] not do it. That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: What advice do you have for your children, grandchildren, future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: The advice I have? Enjoy life. Do the best you can for-- for yourself, without hurting other people. Have fun. Have fun. Have fun. And like Fritz said to me, who was my father-in-law, surround yourself this beautiful things. The ugly come on their own.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message would you now like to deliver to the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no messages for the world. The world-- I mean, who's going to listen to me? I mean, we-- we always thought that, never going to happen, anything. And it does happen, all the time. All the time, just this minute too. What message do I have to give them? It repeats itself. History repeats itself constantly, constantly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody yesterday-- a gentleman said to me-- a friend of mine said, do you think Adolf Hitler was human? I said, yes, he was. How can you call him human? He's a beast! He's an animal. I said, no, Mitch, he's not. The problem is that he's-- he was human. He was a man, like you and anybody else. This is what's dangerous about people. He looked human. Eichmann looked human. Mengele looked human. They were probably good-- good fathers and good family men, loved their wives and children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the whole theory that's genetic with Germans or something, in this new book, I don't believe in it either. Because I have seen Latvians behave far worse than Germans. It is very hard to judge. One goes-- I have been to Germany now and Berlin. I have never felt so at home, because every street was named after Einstein, after Tucholsky after Mendelssohn. I mean, it's not 42nd Street and 43rd Street. Those streets have Jewish personalities. The most glorious synagogues-- Oranienburger Strasse [? they built-- ?] when I go-- went there to the bookshops, all my favorite guys where there, which I didn't read. I mean, Feuchtwanger, and [? Max ?] [? Broder, ?] [? Erich ?] [? Kessner, ?] and Kurt Tucholsky, all those things. I was-- I mean, Germany! And there were-- I-- I mean, everywhere I went was a monument to the Jews-- where the Jews were collected then deported, where they went to Riga from and how many. And it was tended with flowers and clean and beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know what happened. We were all in the same mess. To have grown up in Germany under Hitler, being a German, one didn't have many choices. It's not that they could go out and protest. I mean, the ones who did they beheaded in Plotzensee. Yeah? So it was not, why didn't they do anything? Why didn't they-- it's very easy to say [INAUDIBLE] from New York. But to live under that kind of rule with that threat and where your child could report you when he has-- once he has joined the Hitlerjugend-- it wasn't easy. It wasn't easy at all. So I have no complaints that-- that so-and-so person didn't go out and march and protest. As a matter of fact, in the beginning, the-- the 800 Jewish wives of-- sorry-- 800 German wives of Jewish husbands who were arrested were demonstrating in a certain plaza, surrounded the police. They demonstrated till they let their husbands go. What could the-- soldiers wouldn't shoot the women. They did those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there is a wonderful book, how the aristocracy were fighting the Hitler-- I mean, the Army, the big-- the big armies of [INAUDIBLE] and all that, they were not very, very favorable to Hitler. Well so, this is a different story. And I cannot go out and say, a bad German is-- a good German is a dead German. I know the games which were played. I know how the-- how the Hungarians behaved and how the Latvians behaved and how some of our Jewish people behaved in order to save their lives. If your life are at stake, you sell your own grandmother, which they often did. If you-- well, it's happened. And your children you give it away, because this-- the urge to live is very strong. So I don't sit in judgment of anybody. Just do the best you can. And don't hurt anybody. And don't hate anybody. And no prejudices, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] please. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --on behalf of the Shoah Foundation.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Glad, most glad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my family, my father, Chaim Saks; my mom, Sophie Saks; my little sister, Miriam. And I am 10 years old. Miriam was-- must be in '33 or '34.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Libau, in our yard, in our backyard. It was the jasmine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really know. Somebody sent it to me after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my-- my father stands in the back. My mom is on my right side, I think. And Uncle Boris--

speaker: interviewer
text: Left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uncle Boris--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- my mother's on the left side? All right. And my Uncle Boris is sitting. We are in the park in Libau. I don't know how old I am, must be two, three years old. Must have been cold. I wear a shawl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and my mom. And I-- I am here with my dolly. I remember the beads. They were red, strangely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the color of the bow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Red. Definitely red.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the names of the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim Saks and Sophie Saks. And I'm [INAUDIBLE] Saks or Shoshana.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my youngest brother of my father, Uncle [? Isaac. ?] He was a very promising portrait painter and illustrator. And he loved frescoes. His room was covered with strange plants-- the walls, the-- the ceiling. He slept during the day and worked at night. He was very amusing man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That is Uncle David. He lived in Pretoria. And this is the portrait he did of-- of Smuts, who was president of South Africa or-- I think it was Smuats, S-M-U-A-T-S. And Uncle David lived there for many years. Still, when he heard what happened to the Holocaust, he got a heart-attack and died.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right after the war. Right after the war, when he heard what happened to his family. He was a lovely man. He always brought dogs and cats from the park, all the strays. My-- mother-- grandma's house was full of strays, which was very amusing to us kids. Yeah, This is my young aunt, Aunt Minna. Minna, in old German, means 'love,' by the way, [GERMAN]. And she was extremely talented, extremely beautiful, extremely nice, extremely fabulous. And I loved her very much. She got executed in [? Strasshof ?] together with her lover, Professor [? Lowish, ?] hand-in-hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell-- I just-- well, Minna had-- was a cripple. I mean, we called it a cripple. She had only one leg. And one leg was amputated. And she was unmarried. But then, she met Professor [? Lowish, ?] who was a professor of mine from my school. And they fell in love. And when we got deported to-- to the concentration camp, he did not leave her. He joined her. And when she was going to execution, he accompanied her. That was their marriage, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was a gymnasium-- the first class in high school. This was [INAUDIBLE] gymnasiums, at the time of the Soviets. It was already belong to the state. This is my class. We wear uniforms. It's-- the hats are uniform. And the front, it's Professor [? Lahn, ?] this is the math teacher. And next to him, [INAUDIBLE] the left, is Dr. Greenfeld, who was our principal. And in the middle in the back, between two-- two girls, I peek. That's-- that's I. [INAUDIBLE] Schwartz, who was executed by Baumgartner, sits right next to my best girlfriend, right next to Dr. Greenfeld. There are hardly anybody alive anymore of those people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Oskar Velkin. He was a kid from my class. We went together to school since kindergarten. He was very, very bright and still is very bright. He was deported to Siberia at the outbreak of the war by the Russians, because his parents were, so-called, bourgeois, which was dangerous. And-- but he was lucky enough to go to-- to college in Russia, in Leningrad. He became an architect and still lives in-- in Minsk with his wife and daughter and grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This young man was called [? Micki ?] [? Bernitz. ?] He hails from England. But his father built a factory of carpeting in our town. And he joined our class kind of late. He was a wonderful cellist, a wonderful musician. And I considered him a boyfriend, what, in those days, was considered boyfriend. We rode the bikes together and did homework together. And he was executed in front of the-- in front of his mom, one day in-- in July.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My best girlfriends and I-- I am on the left, am I-- with the light-- light-colored dress. And behind my shoulders, Ella [? Mauer ?] was the smartest kid in our class. Next to Ella is Hilda Saks, who was a pianist, who was-- did to the piano what [? Micki ?] did to the cello. And this is [INAUDIBLE], who was the most extraordinarily, beautiful, fine little girl, whom-- whom they executed one afternoon, because they got the order to do that. And they excused-- Baumgartner excused himself before they shot her. Sorry about that, but I have to kill you this afternoon, he said-- allegedly. That's what the other Jews told me. And this is the last picture we have together, because Hilda went to-- off to her father to Cape Town, South Africa. And she was lucky enough to get on the last boat out of Europe and made it to Cape Town. She's now living in London and is a pianist.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in London. And Hilda gave it to me. Here is an old picture of my mom and her-- her brother and two sisters. Tante Minna is not here there. There is Tante Paula, who lived in Danzig and wore a pince-nez I like to say the word 'pince-nez.' It sounds so French-- very intimidating. And this is Tante [? Johanna, ?] who was quite mad, because of scarlatina. It affected her brain. She spent most of her time in-- in institutions before-- and this is my Uncle Boris, who turned into a Dane overnight, when he thought being Jewish was so dangerous. And that's my mom. I think she wasn't married yet there. They look rather young.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you think this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Libau.

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm, I think my-- an aunt sent it to me after the war. My father's sister lived in America, in Washington. I think she-- she sent it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt Minna sits. She was an invalid, as I said. She was-- must have been a teenager at the time. My mom stands behind her. The two ladies are my father's sister. In the middle, is Tante [INAUDIBLE], who lived in [INAUDIBLE], married to Uncle-- whoever that name was. And this is Tante [? Johanna, ?] the one who was in-- in Washington, who was--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]-- yeah. They-- very much-- yes. They were all very beautiful girls. But they married unhappily. They were just married off to strangers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's on the bottom?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. My-- my Aunt Minna's in the middle. Who those kid are, I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- my mom, my sister, and I visited the relatives in [INAUDIBLE], just because those two gentleman came on a visit from South Africa. Those were the years when there was a big [INAUDIBLE] from-- from the Baltics to South Africa. Like, Abba Eban for example. He hails from Lithuania. And this is my cousin, [? Hersch, ?] the blondie on my mother's side. And the other guy, the other cousin, his name I forgot. Miriam, here, stands in the middle. And I am the-- the other girl there. This is in [INAUDIBLE], [HEBREW], in Jewish, one summer day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my Tante [? Pusch, ?] for Dr. [? Wienrech, ?] was my-- my teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's who's on the left-- Tante [? Pusch ?] for Dr. [? Wienrech, ?] who was a teacher of our school. She had us over for tea, her favorite gals, the creme-de-la-creme, like Miss [INAUDIBLE]. I stand next to her, tall and to the middle. Next to me is [? Silla-- ?] [? Silla, ?] who is of my class. And before [? Silla ?] is a little girl called [? Sarote, ?] who has come-- who has emigrated to Palestina and came on a visit. And next to Sarote is my [? Laine ?] [INAUDIBLE] and Ella [? Mauer. ?] And we are in-- in Libau on a Sunday afternoon. It was a lovely day.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me and my grandma on an outing in the woods. Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhere outside Libau. In the-- it's in the woods. We have been somewhere-- the country. My grandma is usually-- is very, very-- in a very good mood. And I'm so proud of her and taller than her too. I loved my grandma. She was the greatest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Lina. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Lina ?] Saks-- oh, [? Kamza-- ?] oh, [? Stusser. ?] She had quite a few marriages. She was a tremendous girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: So could you tell us about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah! That was a wonderful afternoon with my Aunt Minna. And one of her boyfriends took the picture. And we were at the-- at the Libau [INAUDIBLE]. And it's a port. We had a beautiful harbor there. It was really great. I can smell the water when I look at the picture. And we had such a good time. My-- my Tante Minna wore a electric-blue silk dress. And she had a carved ebony rose right in her collar. And I wore a pink dress with black dots. And I had terrible eczema. Look how I hide my hands. That's what saved my life. Probably had lots of fish this morning. And I really remember that day. I really had a good time with my Aunt Minna. We listened to records together and sang all the latest hits. It was great.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I remember who-- the one who took this picture. His name was [? Itroff. ?] He was a hypnotist. I don't know why he was a hypnotist. But he was it by profession. Tried to hypnotize my mom, didn't succeed. And here, I wear my school uniform. It's, I think, the last pictures I have of me. Tante Minna is in the back. And my little Miriam is sitting on the window sill. And this is looking out from the living room of our house. That's my mom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sophie. Sophie-- looks good here. I remember that dress. It was made out of [INAUDIBLE] which was the hot [INAUDIBLE] of those years. OK, I'm very proud and happy to introduce my family. There is Dror, my son--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and [INAUDIBLE], his wife; and Sassy, my little Saskia. And this is Sabella. That she had birthday yesterday. Yesterday, she had birthday, a whole year. Yeah! That's so funny! And Ezi, is not here. He lives-- he's in the city today. But he's a 17-year-old Ezra here. He's very nice. And oh! This is my husband. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Without him, there wouldn't have been a family, right? That's Emanuel. And that's about it. I'm very happy to [NON-ENGLISH-SPEECH] Thank you all.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that is funny, isn't it? Isn't it funny? Isn't it funny?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]
